---
title: "inflationkit"
subtitle: "Inflation decomposition, persistence, and Phillips curves in R"
author: "Charles Coverdale"
institute: "London, United Kingdom"
date: "2026-05-01"
date-format: "MMMM YYYY"
format:
  beamer:
    aspectratio: 169
    fontsize: 11pt
    navigation: empty
    theme: default
    colortheme: default
    fonttheme: default
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    include-in-header: header.tex
    slide-level: 2
    keep-tex: false
    fig-align: center
    classoption:
      - t
---


## The question

\vspace{1.5em}

\begin{center}
\Large\bfseries\color{econblue}
How does an applied economist run the standard\\[0.1em]
inflation diagnostic playbook in one R package?
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}

\begin{center}
\normalsize\itshape
Eleven functions, one \texttt{ik\_} prefix, every dataset in any country.
\end{center}

::: notes
Open with the question, not the package. Inflation analysis splits into
five workflows that have lived in disconnected scripts and spreadsheets:
decomposition, core measures, persistence, Phillips curves, forecast
evaluation. inflationkit unifies them. 30 seconds.
:::


## Why it matters

- **Central banks** (Fed, ECB, BoE) report core, trim, persistence and Phillips curve estimates in every quarterly briefing
- **Sovereign treasuries and OBR-equivalents** track breakeven inflation and forecast bias for fiscal projections
- **Research departments** (NBER, CEPR, IMF) need replicable persistence and slope estimates across countries
- **Financial press and policy analysts** decompose headline spikes into supply versus demand each release day

::: notes
Four distinct stakeholder groups, four routine use cases. Point at the
2021-2023 episode: every central bank ran exactly this playbook. 60 seconds.
:::


## What is already out there

- **`Inflation`** (CRAN): four core measures only, no decomposition, persistence, Phillips curves, or forecast evaluation; unmaintained since 2017\footnote{\scriptsize Iturbide \& Tovar (2017), \textit{Inflation: Core Inflation}, R package v1.4.}
- **Spreadsheet workbooks**: every applied team has its own; no version control, no testing, no citation
- **Bespoke scripts**: persistence and Phillips curve code is rewritten in every paper
- **Proprietary platforms** (Haver, Bloomberg): closed source, no transparency, no reproducibility

\vspace{0.5em}

The gap: **no single CRAN package covers the standard inflation workflow end to end**.

::: notes
Respect the existing CRAN package; it is narrow but solid for what it
covers. The gap is breadth and currency, not quality. 45 seconds.
:::


## What inflationkit offers

1. **Coverage**: 11 functions across decomposition, core, persistence, Phillips curves, trend extraction, breakeven, and forecast evaluation
2. **Interface**: every function accepts arbitrary column names and returns an S3 object with a `print()` method; `rbind()` composes panels
3. **Provenance**: pure R, no compiled code, no API calls, no bundled data; over 200 tests; CRAN since March 2026

\vspace{0.5em}

Methods follow @bryan1994measuring (core), @pivetta2007persistence (persistence), @stock2007why (trend), @mincer1969evaluation (forecast eval)\footnote{\scriptsize Bryan \& Cecchetti (1994), \textit{Measuring core inflation}, NBER WP 4303.}.

::: notes
Three bullets, three selling points. Pure-computation design lets users
plug ONS, Eurostat, BLS, ABS data straight in. CRAN presence signals
maturity. 45 seconds.
:::


## Decomposition: what is driving headline inflation

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.65\textheight]{figures/fig2_decompose.pdf}
\end{center}

\vspace{-0.4em}

{\footnotesize\itshape Component contributions sum to headline. Energy and transport drove the 2021-2022 peak; housing contributes slowly throughout. Exports: \texttt{ik\_decompose()}, \texttt{ik\_diffusion()}.}
```

::: notes
First family. The component stack is the first slide of any inflation
briefing. Pandemic peak energy-led, persistent housing component
underneath. 25 seconds.
:::


## Core measures: four ways to strip the noise

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.65\textheight]{figures/fig3_core_comparison.pdf}
\end{center}

\vspace{-0.4em}

{\footnotesize\itshape Trimmed mean, weighted median, exclusion, asymmetric trim. Distribution-based measures attenuate the spike more than fixed exclusion. Exports: \texttt{ik\_core()}, \texttt{ik\_sticky\_flexible()}.}
```

::: notes
Second family. Bryan-Cecchetti programme: distribution-based cores beat
ex-food-and-energy because the high-variance components shift period
to period. Cleveland Fed has published trim CPI for 30 years. 25 seconds.
:::


## Phillips curves: slack, expectations, and the slope

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.65\textheight]{figures/fig4_phillips.pdf}
\end{center}

\vspace{-0.4em}

{\footnotesize\itshape Traditional, expectations-augmented, and hybrid New Keynesian forms with HAC standard errors. Exports: \texttt{ik\_phillips()}, \texttt{ik\_persistence()}, \texttt{ik\_breakeven()}.}
```

::: notes
Third family. Phillips curve is weakly identified in aggregate data
(Mavroeidis et al 2014). Single-equation OLS is a starting diagnostic;
structural inference needs cross-sectional designs. 25 seconds.
:::


## Central formulas

\vspace{0.3em}

\textbf{Persistence} (sum of AR coefficients):
\begin{equation}
\rho \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{p} \phi_j, \qquad \pi_t = c + \sum_{j=1}^{p}\phi_j \pi_{t-j} + \varepsilon_t
\end{equation}

\textbf{Phillips curve} (hybrid New Keynesian):
\begin{equation}
\pi_t = \alpha + \beta_f\,\mathbb{E}_t \pi_{t+1} + \beta_b\,\pi_{t-1} + \gamma\,x_t + \varepsilon_t
\end{equation}

\textbf{Mincer-Zarnowitz} (forecast bias):
\begin{equation}
\pi_t = \alpha + \beta\,\hat{\pi}_t + \varepsilon_t, \qquad H_0:\ (\alpha,\beta) = (0, 1)
\end{equation}

::: notes
Three equations, one idea each. Persistence is one number summarising
mean-reversion speed. Phillips curve nests three specifications. MZ is
the bias test every published forecast should pass. 60 seconds.
:::


## Package at a glance

::: columns
::: {.column width="58%"}

Function families:

- **Decomposition**: `ik_decompose`, `ik_core`, `ik_sticky_flexible`, `ik_diffusion`
- **Dynamics**: `ik_persistence`, `ik_phillips`
- **Trend**: `ik_trend` (HP, BN, exponential, MA)
- **Breakeven**: `ik_breakeven`
- **Forecast eval**: `ik_forecast_eval` (MZ, Nordhaus, DM)
- **Helpers**: `ik_compare`, `ik_sample_data`

Deps: `cli`, `grDevices`, `graphics`, `stats`. R $\geq$ 4.1.0.

:::
::: {.column width="40%"}

\begin{block}{Flexible column names}
Every function accepts:

\texttt{date\_col} | \texttt{item\_col} | \texttt{weight\_col} | \texttt{price\_col}

ONS, Eurostat, BLS, ABS data flows in without reshaping.
\end{block}

:::
:::

::: notes
Two columns. Eleven exports across five families. The flexible-column
interface is the practical pitch: data from any agency, no reshaping
needed. 45 seconds.
:::


## Minimal working example

```r
library(inflationkit)

# Five-line canonical workflow
d <- ik_sample_data("components")

ik_decompose(d)                                          # contributions
ik_core(d, method = "trimmed_mean", trim = 0.08)         # core
ik_persistence(headline_series)                          # half-life
ik_phillips(inflation = infl, slack = output_gap,        # PC slope
            type = "traditional", robust_se = "HAC")
```

\footnotesize
Each call returns an S3 object with a `print()` method. Outputs compose
via `rbind()` for panel-style analysis across countries or vintages.

::: notes
Live-read the code. Five lines, five S3 objects, every diagnostic a
central bank reports. The HAC option is the default robustness adjust-
ment for serially-correlated residuals. 60 seconds.
:::


## Case study: the pandemic-era playbook on US CPI

**Data.** FRED series \texttt{CPIAUCSL} (headline) and \texttt{CPILFESL} (less food and energy), monthly, 2015 to 2026.

**Question.** *Was the 2021-2023 spike a relative-price shock or a regime shift in trend inflation?*

**Why this case.** Largest test of inflation targeting in three decades. Every central bank ran exactly this diagnostic sequence in real time\footnote{\scriptsize Reis (2022), \textit{The burst of high inflation}, NBER WP 30616. Shapiro (2022), \textit{Decomposing supply and demand driven inflation}, FRBSF WP.}.

::: notes
Setup slide. No results. Name the data, the period, the question. Point
forward to the hero figure. 45 seconds.
:::


## US CPI, 2015 to 2026: headline, core, and HP trend

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.68\textheight]{figures/fig6_fred.pdf}
\end{center}

\vspace{-0.2em}

{\footnotesize\itshape Headline (grey) peaks above 9 per cent in June 2022; core (blue) peaks at 6.7 per cent. HP trend (red dashed) captures the regime shift. \texttt{ik\_persistence()} on year-over-year headline returns sum-of-AR = 0.97. Source: FRED.}
```

::: notes
The money slide. Ninety seconds. Walk through the three series:
headline spike, core attenuation, trend regime shift. The 0.97
persistence reading is exactly what Stock-Watson 2007 warn about:
unfiltered inflation looks near-permanent because the local mean
has drifted. Pair persistence with trend before drawing conclusions.
Pause before moving on.
:::


## Mincer-Zarnowitz: a naive AR(1) forecast was biased through the spike

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.68\textheight]{figures/fig5_mz.pdf}
\end{center}

\vspace{-0.5em}

{\footnotesize\itshape Realised inflation against an AR(1)-with-drift forecast, 120 months synthetic data. Slope below unity, intercept off zero: under-reaction and bias. \texttt{ik\_forecast\_eval()} returns the test statistic and joint $p$-value.}
```

::: notes
Secondary analytic. The same package that decomposed and trended
inflation also evaluates whether your forecast caught the spike.
Most simple time-series forecasts under-reacted in 2021-2022. MZ is
the standard pre-commitment test before publishing any forecast. 60 seconds.
:::


## What inflationkit does not yet do

- **Single-equation OLS only**: no IV, no GMM, no @hazell2022slope cross-sectional design
- **No bundled price data**: pure computation; data still comes from ONS, Eurostat, BLS, ABS
- **No regional decomposition**: the package operates at the country level
- **No DSGE-style structural inference**: that is the job of `dynare4r` or bespoke Stan code

\vspace{0.5em}

**v0.2.0 roadmap**: regional Phillips curve panel estimators (HSL-style cross-sectional design), expanded breakeven options across maturities, sticky-flexible helpers for the full Atlanta Fed component map.

::: notes
Honest limitations. The aggregate Phillips curve slope is weakly
identified; the package fits the diagnostic OLS but flags this in
the documentation. Frame v0.2.0 as a commitment. 45 seconds.
:::


## Contact, code, paper

::: columns
::: {.column width="62%"}

```{=latex}
{\large\bfseries Charles Coverdale\par}
\vspace{0.5em}
\texttt{charles.f.coverdale@gmail.com}\\
\texttt{github.com/charlescoverdale/inflationkit}\\
\texttt{charlescoverdale.github.io/publications}
\vspace{0.9em}

\textbf{Acknowledgements.} CPI data: Federal Reserve Bank of St.\ Louis (FRED, public). Methodological debts to Bryan, Cecchetti, Pivetta, Stock, Watson, Mincer, Zarnowitz, Diebold, Mariano, Hodrick, Prescott, and the Cleveland Fed trim-CPI team. Thanks to CRAN maintainers and reviewers of \emph{inflationkit} v0.1.0.
```

:::
::: {.column width="35%"}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth]{figures/qrcode_paper.png}

\vspace{0.3em}

{\footnotesize\itshape Scan for paper PDF}
\end{center}

:::
:::

::: notes
Close cleanly. Name, email, GitHub, publications URL. QR code points
straight to the paper PDF. Acknowledge the methodological lineage.
Open for questions.
:::
